AI/ML Architecture Design</div></div></div></span></td><td class="calendar-td"><span class="presentation-cal-links"><span class="hide-med hide-small"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess143.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess143"></a></span></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess143 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=112743">Sitao Huang</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=304843">Minah Lee</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>As AI workloads continue to diversify across graph learning, diffusion-based generation, visual computing, and large-scale neural inference, accelerator research is increasingly shaped by the need for both domain-specific optimization and more systematic hardware design methodologies. This session brings together work that spans specialized accelerators for heterogeneous graph neural networks, diffusion models, and Gaussian splatting, along with innovations in GPU microarchitecture, approximate arithmetic, numeric formats, and automated DNN accelerator generation. Several papers highlight algorithm-architecture co-design, showing how workload structure, temporal reuse, sparsity, and token reduction can be directly translated into hardware efficiency gains. Others focus on more general architectural foundations, including register-file coordination, low-power approximate multipliers, and flexible low-precision formats for emerging AI models. The session also includes work on end-to-end design automation, reflecting the growing importance of frameworks that can rapidly explore, generate, and map accelerators for a broad range of workloads and quantization schemes. Collectively, these papers illustrate the breadth of current AI hardware research, where both specialized acceleration techniques and scalable design methods are essential to meeting the performance, energy, and programmability demands of next-generation intelligent systems.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" Hassan Najafi</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>The growing diversity of AI models, from large language models (LLMs) to graph analytics and neural rendering, places increasing pressure on hardware architectures to support heterogeneous computational patterns efficiently. This session presents nine papers exploring architectural mechanisms that improve performance, efficiency, and flexibility across a wide range of emerging AI workloads. These papers demonstrate ongoing efforts to design flexible yet efficient AI hardware architectures that address the growing heterogeneity of modern machine learning workloads while maintaining high performance and energy efficiency across datacenter, edge, and mobile platforms.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr This includes solving differential equations, graph pattern mining, scientific computing and genome analysis workflows. Of course, AI cannot be forgotten and is there too. Papers in this session leverage a wide range of technologies from foundry SRAM of today and stacked flash solutions  to up-and-coming RRAM and memristive technologies.  This session highlights the breadth of areas that memory-centric computing will impact and seeks an answer to the often repeated question of "are we there yet?"</div><div class="info-section width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess163 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=614843">Krishnendu Chakrabarty</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=876743">Jeff Dyck</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Advances in proof-centric RTL verification are showcased across agentic reasoning, scalable SAT/IC3, and high-throughput equivalence and model checking. Topics include a reasoning-enhanced programming agent, parallel SAT solving, and IC3 optimizations that cut SAT effort per obligation, complemented by data-driven lemma generation. Scalable combinational equivalence checking is addressed via miter-aware LUT mapping, parallel sweeping-based scheduling, and factored-form sharing. The session also covers complementary approximate reachability, invariant-driven verification of RISC-V cryptography extensions, and tensor-centric verification for domain-specific accelerators - highlighting how better structure and parallelism improve robustness and throughput on large designs.<br></div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T17:42:00Z" psid="sess163" s_utc="2026-07-29T17:30:00Z" ssid="RESEARCH142"><td class="presentation-time-td"><span class="left-cal-links"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess163--RESEARCH142.ics --><a class="ical-link" The papers explore dynamic runtime and scheduling strategies for heterogeneous architectures, smarter resource orchestration for Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, and advanced methodologies that strengthen the verifiability and security of real-world deployments. Together, these contributions bridge the gap between high-level AI algorithms and efficient, secure hardware execution.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=853843">Bhaskar Vedula</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session presents shiftâleft hardware security techniques for exposing vulnerabilities before tapeout, with a focus on side-channel leakage in cryptographic IPs and edge systems. The topics cover simulation-based and MLâassisted analysis to detect power, timing, and analog anomalies in  designs. They also present  lightweight architectural defenses for securing off-chip interfaces. Together, these works show how integrating security analysis early in the design flow reduces risk in silicon design oriented towards edge AI and embedded hardware.				</div><div class="info-section width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess171 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=446143">Antonino Tumeo</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>As Mooreâs Law faces the challenges of extreme technology scaling and increasing model complexity, the burden of system reliability is shifting from hardware to intelligent, full-stack orchestration. This session explores the cutting edge of resilient and predictable computing, spanning deep learning accelerators, cloud microservices, and time-sensitive networks. We navigate the 'Outlier Dichotomy' in LLMs and soft errors in NPUs, introducing micro-architectural safeguards that bridge the gap between safety-critical requirements and performance. Beyond reliability, we tackle the 'black box' of GPU warp scheduling and the interference dynamics of SMT-enabled cloud servers. From adaptive ECC for SSDs to thermal-aware task scaling on Edge TPUs, these papers present a unified vision: achieving microsecond-level precision and robust fault tolerance without sacrificing the efficiency demanded by modern AI workloads.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T17:43:00Z" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=564843">Siyuan Niu</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>As quantum computing advances toward system-scale deployment, the transition from individual qubits to robust, scalable architectures has emerged as a central challenge. This special session, "Quantum Stack Attack: Building the Machine," brings together leading experts from industry, national laboratories, and academia to examine the foundational innovations driving the next phase of quantum computing. The session highlights four complementary directions essential to building practical quantum systems: high-fidelity hybrid analogâdigital simulation on Google's Willow processor; benchmarking and modeling of early fault-tolerant architectures; development of high-precision quantum control techniques for complex multi-qubit devices; and integration of AI-assisted methodologies for design automation and system optimization. By connecting progress across these layers of the quantum stack, the session presents a unified perspective on the engineering principles and computational tools required to move beyond NISQ-era demonstrations. Attendees will gain insight into emerging methodologies reshaping quantum system design and understand how these advances collectively define a roadmap toward reliable, next-generation quantum computing with broad impact across science, technology, and industry.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess110 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=604843">Chaojian Li</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=573443">Zishen Wan</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>As AI workloads expand beyond conventional GPU-centric execution, rethinking compute, data movement, and system robustness across layers becomes essential. This session presents nine works that explore cross-layer co-design spanning hardware, architecture, compiler, and algorithm levels. Topics include adaptive communication compression for distributed GNNs, optical neural networks with robustness-aware mapping, chiplet-based accelerator exploration, and compiler-driven data movement reduction for PIM architectures. Novel dataflow paradigms, heterogeneous fine-tuning strategies, distribution-aware sparsity, and multi-instance GPU inference further highlight how decoupling computation, data, and deployment constraints enables efficient and resilient AI systems across emerging platforms and challenging environments.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=733543">Dave Rich</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>As modern chips evolve into highly integrated systems combining hardware, software, security, and heterogeneous compute clusters, verification must expand beyond block-level correctness. Robust validation now demands coordinated simulation, emulation, prototyping, and HW/SW co-verification strategies to ensure full-system integrity under diverse workloads. Join us in learning about some of the latest techniques to address this, and discover how to confidently verify the integrated systems of the future.				</div><div class="info-section The papers in this session address diverse problems, including drift-resilient compensation for RRAM-based in-memory computing, diffusion-based analysis of long-tailed wafer defects, and operator-aware scheduling for fault-tolerant AI accelerators. Additional works focus on improving test efficiency through scan activity probability modeling for programmable low-power LBIST and ATPG-free self-supervised learning for test point insertion. Advances in lithographic SEM contour extraction and LLM-driven assertion generation with fault criticality evaluation further demonstrate the growing role of data-driven methods in validation and safety analysis.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T20:42:00Z" psid="sess160" We need to overcome memory bandwidth, data movement, and energy-efficiency bottlenecks but how? Through hardware-software co-design spanning algorithms, architectures, circuits, and system scheduling. These papers explore complementary innovations including low-bit and nonlinear quantization, sparse attention execution, DRAM-PIM co-execution with minimal interference, systematic CIM dataflow design-space exploration, and more efficient signed-weight mapping and activation conversion for ReRAM/SRAM-based CIM.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess133 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=948743">Zichang He</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=724843">Hafiz  Mohammad Hasan Babu</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Scalable quantum computing requires coordinated advances in circuit synthesis, compilation, and algorithmic optimization. This session features exact T and CNOT gate synthesis engines, hypergraph-based circuit optimizers, and architecture aware compilation for neutral atom systems, all aimed at improving scalability and qubit locality. It also highlights hardware calibrated cost models for hybrid neural architecture search, Hamiltonian guided pretraining for variational algorithms, and efficient time dependent Hamiltonian solvers. Together, these works show how rigorous synthesis, intelligent search, and hardware aware optimization combine into a unified automation stack that accelerates scalable, performance driven quantum circuit and algorithm design. Ultimately, these advances point toward a future where quantum software design becomes not only automated and scalable, but capable of unlocking algorithmic possibilities far beyond todayâs imagination.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T20:42:00Z" psid="sess133" We delve into the architectural breakthroughsâfrom analog accelerators to neuromorphic spiking networksâthat allow us to scale complex AI onto the edge. The papers in this session show new approaches to optimize the "brain-to-body" connection of Physical AI, turning massive vision-language models into lean, lightning-fast engines for the real class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=364843">Kathy Grise</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>The Next Decade of Semiconductors and Transformation Through Industry, Academia, and Government is a panel bringing together leaders from industry, academia, and government to discuss the trends shaping semiconductor innovation and the ecosystem required to scale it.<br><br>The panel will focus on four major themes defining the next decade: EDA and evolving design methodologies, including the growing role of open-source ecosystems; advanced memory and storage architectures needed to support AI-driven data movement and processing; post-silicon advances that extend performance beyond traditional scaling; and the future of semiconductor manufacturing and workforce readiness, including stronger fab connectivity, talent pipelines, and skills development to sustain global competitiveness and long-term innovation.<br><br>This panel session is organized by IEEE Global Semiconductors.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T22:00:00Z" psid="sess288" clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="add to Flagged" value="add"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_plus" class="icon-linklings-plus"><g fill="red" stroke="white" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle stroke="red" cx="12" cy="12" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess255 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype108" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=000343">Bill Lin</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>AI systems are rapidly outgrowing the capabilities of purely electronic interconnects and compute substrates. As models scale toward multi-chiplet and ultra-large distributed systems, the energy, latency, and bandwidth limits of electrical signaling have become critical bottlenecks. Integrated photonics, with its ultra-high bandwidth, low latency, and energy-efficient communication, is essential for sustaining AI's growth. However, realizing the full potential of large-scale Electronic-Photonic Integrated Chiplets (EPICs) requires a unified and mature Electronic-Photonic Design Automation (EPDA) ecosystem. Currently, EPDA is fragmented and lacks the mature co-design, co-simulation, auto-layout, verification, and design-for-manufacturability (DFM) infrastructure that enabled VLSI's growth. Establishing EPDA as a first-class design paradigm is both urgent and foundational.<br><br>This special session brings together leading researchers and industry innovators to explore the emerging EPDA landscape from design to silicon. The session will feature three key talks:<br><br>1. Lightmatter will present advanced electro-optic co-simulation frameworks for wafer-scale photonic fabrics, demonstrating how tightly co-designed electronics and photonics can overcome bandwidth and energy bottlenecks in next-generation multi-chiplet AI systems.<br><br>2. Prof. Jiaqi Gu (ASU) will discuss breakthroughs in automated placement/routing and AI-driven design-for-manufacturability for photonic inverse design, addressing scalability and yield challenges in EPIC layout synthesis.<br><br>3. AIM Photonics will offer a foundry-level perspective on heterogeneous integration, advanced packaging, and PDK evolution, focusing on process-design co-optimization and ecosystem development for manufacturable EPIC technologies.<br><br>These talks highlight photonics' rising importance in heterogeneous, chiplet-based AI systems and the transformative role of EPDA in enabling scalable, fabrication-ready EPICs. This session is timely for DAC and aims to catalyze deeper co-evolution across academia, industry, and foundries.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span By embedding intelligence -- from fine-tuned LLMs to in-storage Hyperdimensional Computing -- directly into the hardware fabric, these works tackle the 'Data Movement Wall' head-on. Spanning Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) acceleration, Ethereum analytics, and MRAM-based BNNs, this session redefines memory as a cognitive engine. It marks a paradigm shift toward autonomous, energy-efficient silicon capable of processing massive AI workloads at the source, unlocking performance for next-generation intelligent systems.</div><div width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess118 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=227143">Yibo Lin</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=782843">Weidong Cao</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>This session presents a shift from manual layout and iterative tuning toward autonomous design agents and differentiable optimization for analog and RF systems. Featured works include LLM-driven PCB schematic generation, Monte Carlo tree search for analog placement, and diffusion-based priors for transistor sizing. By leveraging physics-constrained inverse design and stochastic knowledge elicitation, these approaches address the challenges due to advanced nodes. Collectively, they pave the way for a self-evolving design flow, significantly reducing the productivity gap in complex circuit synthesis while maintaining high-fidelity performance constraints.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T22:42:00Z" psid="sess118" SoC, Heterogeneous, and Reconfigurable Architectures</div></div></div></span></td><td class="calendar-td"><span class="presentation-cal-links"><span class="hide-med hide-small"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess174.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess174"></a></span></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: #cc0000; width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess174 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=621843">Peipei Zhou</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=271143">Rosario Cammarota</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span><br>This session explores how unconventional computing paradigmsâfrom cryptographic randomness to probabilistic fluctuationsâare being harnessed to build secure, resilient systems. The session begins by addressing the computational bottlenecks of Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP) and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC). The two opening papers introduce an end-to-end FPGA-accelerated ZKP system and a pioneering photonic-electronic engine for Merkle trees. These are followed by hardware acceleration for zkVM front-end trace generation, a unified ISA for flexible elliptic curve multiplication, and a high-throughput framework for algebraic proof systems on edge devices. The next four papers  focus on Stochastic and Probabilistic Computing, where "noise" is transformed into a computational resource. They examine a novel stochastic MAC architecture, a low-latency architecture for counter-based stochastic computing,  an RRAM-based architecture for combinatorial optimization, and  a self-compensating p-bit unit for Gaussian noise generation. The session concludes with a co-optimization framework that harnesses the inherent fault tolerance of Diffusion Models for energy-efficient AI inference.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr class="agenda-item" e_utc="2026-07-29T22:42:00Z" psid="sess174" s_utc="2026-07-29T22:30:00Z" ssid="RESEARCH2504"><td class="presentation-time-td"><span class="left-cal-links"><span style="display: none;"><!-- cal-file sess174--RESEARCH2504.ics --><a class="ical-link" href="/wp-content/plugins/linklings_wp_program/get_cal.php?id=sess174--RESEARCH2504"></a></span><span class="agenda-add-button agenda-button clickable" name="add_to_agenda" role="switch" style="color: This session highlights transformative optimization approaches that break free from traditional 2D standard-cell constraints to address the "More than Moore" era. Attendees will discover pioneering research on true-3D and mixed-size analytical global placement for face-to-face 3D ICs, addressing the critical challenges of 3D timing closure and macro orientation. Furthermore, the session showcases novel methodologies that dive deeper into the layout hierarchy, featuring cell-structure-independent transistor-level placement. Alongside these architectural shifts, the session will feature state-of-the-art analytical engines that integrate reinforcement learning, differentiable timing-driven models, and IR-drop awareness to squeeze optimal power, performance, and area (PPA) out of next-generation layouts.</div><div class="info-section session-contents"><span class="info-label">Presentations</span><table><tr width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess140 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chairs</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=542543">Kartik Prabhu</a></div></span><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=824843">Cong Guo</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Rapid large language and diffusion-based model deployment has resulted in major bottlenecks in serving scalable and efficient inference. This session presents architectural and runtime innovations that improve throughput, energy efficiency, and service predictability across modern accelerator platforms. The works span sparsity-aware GPU kernels, metadata-efficient sparse formats, and erasure-coded KV-cache designs to surpass dense execution and reduce load imbalance in distributed serving. Heterogeneous CPUâGPU co-execution and predictive expert caching enable high-throughput Mixture-of-Experts inference, while hardware-aware routing integrates real-time system signals to meet service level objectives. Low-bit adaptive quantization with dedicated microarchitectures further reduces power without sacrificing accuracy. Additional advances accelerate diffusion-based LLMs through phase-aware optimization and speculative decoding and introduce adaptive operator-level tuning frameworks that eliminate redundant compilation overheads for dynamic-shape workloads. Together, these contributions advance scalable, high-efficiency large-model serving through tightly integrated architectural and runtime co-design.</div><div class="info-section width: 30px;" tabindex="0" title="remove from Flagged" value="remove"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" stroke-width="1.5" id="icon_linklings_minus" class="icon-linklings-minus"><g fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-miterlimit="10"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10.5"></circle><path d="m6.27 12h11.46"></path></g></svg></span></span></td></tr><tr class="sess108 slots-slidedown" etypes="sstype101" style="display: none;"><td class="session-display" colspan="7"><div style="display: none;"><div class="info-section session-chairs-list-sect"><span class="info-label">Session Chair</span><span class="session-chair-list"><span class="session-chair"><div class="presenter-name"><a data-link-type="full-program_all.program.chair.person" href="/?post_type=page&p=15&uid=551843">Ayesha Siddique</a></div></span></span></div><div class="info-section session-description"><span class="info-label">Description</span>Description: As post-quantum cryptography, confidential virtualization, and encrypted data processing move from theory to deployment, hardware platforms are becoming the decisive battleground for both leakage and resilience. This DAC session brings together foundational advances and sobering attacks that redefine how we design, verify, and protect next-generation secure systems by presenting methods for significantly reducing side-channel overhead, a modular RISC-V extension tailored for post-quantum cryptography, and AI-inspired architectural techniques that accelerate LPN-based primitives critical to PQC deployments. This session will also shed light on analytical, timing-aware pre-silicon leakage estimation, practical fault-injection attacks targeting FPGA logic synthesis tools, and AI-assisted threat-to-assertion learning to improve SoC security verification coverage. Furthermore, this session will present sparsity-aware optimizations for encrypted sparse matrix multiplication through hardwareâsoftware co-design, processing-in-memory acceleration to scale fully homomorphic encryption databases, and securely migrating workloads across heterogeneous trusted environments without breaking isolation guarantees.</div><div class="info-section
